devanagari: चंद्रवंशवर्णनं बुधस्य जन्म तस्मान्मनुपुत्र्यामिलायां जातस्य पुरूरवस उपाख्यानं च । श्रीशुक उवाच । अथातः श्रूयतां राजन् वंशः सोमस्य पावनः ...
english_devnagari: caṃdravaṃśavarṇanaṃ budhasya janma tasmānmanuputryāmilāyāṃ jātasya purūravasa upākhyānaṃ ca śrīśuka uvāca athātaḥ śrūyatāṃ rājan vaṃśaḥ somasya pāvanaḥ yasminnailādayo bhūpāḥ kīrtyante puṇyakīrtayaḥ

translation:
Śrī Śuka Said:
After this (description of Mithilā kings), now listen to the hallowing race of Soma (the moon-god), in which will be glorified celebrated kings of auspicious fame, like Purūravas, the son of Ilā (Aila),[1] Oh King.
purport:
Footnote [1]:
Although Purūravas is regarded as the son of Ilā, Manu’s daughter, it is probable that this human founder of the Lunar race hailed from Ilāvṛta, the region round-about mount Meru.
madhye tvilāvṛtaṃ nāma mahā-meroḥ samantataḥ /—Matsya Purāṇa. 113.19.
Roughly this corresponds to the region round modern Pamirs. Purūravas and his descendants had contacts with ‘gods’ who are now identified as Aryans who inhabited the northern part of Pamirs or Uttara Kuru—vide D.P. Misra—Proto. Hist. of India, ch. 1.

devanagari: सहस्रशिरसः पुंसो नाभिह्रद सरोरुहात् । जातस्यासीत् सुतो धातुः अत्रिः पितृसमो गुणैः ॥ २ ॥
english_devnagari: sahasraśirasaḥ puṃso nābhihrada saroruhāt jātasyāsīt suto dhātuḥ atriḥ pitṛsamo guṇaiḥ

[aorist active third single], [injunctive active third single]

translation: The creator (god Brahmā), born out of the lotus which was grown out of the deep pool-like navel of the Supreme Person of innumerable (lit. thousands of) heads, had a son called Atri, who was equal to his father in excellent qualities.

devanagari: तस्य दृग्भ्योऽभवत् पुत्रः सोमोऽमृतमयः किल । विप्रौषध्युडुगणानां ब्रह्मणा कल्पितः पतिः ॥ ३ ॥
english_devnagari: tasya dṛgbhyo viprauṣadhyuḍugaṇānāṃ brahmaṇā kalpitaḥ patiḥ
translation: Wonderfully enough a Son called Soma was born out of his tears of joy, and hence he became an embodiment of nectar. He was appointed as the protector (or king) of Brāhmaṇas, (medicinal)plants and herbs and hosts of constellations and stars, by god Brahmā.

devanagari: सोऽयजद् राजसूयेन विजित्य भुवनत्रयम् । पत्‍नीं बृहस्पतेर्दर्पात् तारां नामाहरद् बलात् ॥ ४ ॥
english_devnagari: so pat‍nīṃ bṛhaspaterdarpāt tārāṃ nāmāharad balāt
translation: After conquering the three worlds, he worshipped Lord Viṣṇu by performing a Rājasūya sacrifice. Out of high notions about self-importance, he forcibly abducted Tārā,[2] the wife of Bṛhaspati.
purport:
Footnote [2]:
This is a symbolism, rooted in the vedic doctrine of Soma. For details vide Agrawal—ALP. A study, pp. 130-31.

devanagari: यदा स देवगुरुणा याचितोऽभीक्ष्णशो मदात् । नात्यजत् तत्कृते जज्ञे सुरदानवविग्रहः ॥ ५ ॥
english_devnagari: yadā sa devaguruṇā yācito nātyajat tatkṛte jajñe suradānavavigrahaḥ

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [nominative single from √ yaj class 1 verb], [vocative single from √ yaj class 1 verb], [accusative single from √ yaj class 1 verb]

translation: Even though he was repeatedly and earnestly requested by Bṛhaspati, the preceptor of gods, out of arrogance, he refused to restore her (to Bṛhaspati). On this issue was fought a battle between gods and demons.

devanagari: शुक्रो बृहस्पतेर्द्वेषाद् अग्रहीत् सासुरोडुपम् । हरो गुरुसुतं स्नेहात् सर्वभूतगणावृतः ॥ ६ ॥
english_devnagari: śukro bṛhaspaterdveṣād agrahīt sāsuroḍupam haro gurusutaṃ snehāt sarvabhūtagaṇāvṛtaḥ
translation: Out of hatred against Bṛhaspati, Śukra along with Asuras, espoused the cause of Soma, the Lord of stars, while god Śiva surrounded by his hosts of goblins took the side of Bṛhaspati, his preceptor’s son.[3]
purport:
Footnote [3]:
God Śiva’s preceptor was sage Aṅgiras, the father of Bṛhaspati—Bhāvāratha Dīpikā

devanagari: सर्वदेवगणोपेतो महेन्द्रो गुरुमन्वयात् । सुरासुरविनाशोऽभूत् समरस्तारकामयः ॥ ७ ॥
english_devnagari: sarvadevagaṇopeto mahendro gurumanvayāt surāsuravināśo
translation: The great Indra, accompanied by all the hosts of gods, followed his preceptor (Bṛhaspati). Thus, for the sake of Tārā, a great battle was fought, resulting in the destruction of gods and demons.

devanagari: निवेदितोऽथाङ्गिरसा सोमं निर्भर्त्स्य विश्वकृत् । तारां स्वभर्त्रे प्रायच्छद् अन्तर्वत्‍नीमवैत् पतिः ॥ ८ ॥
english_devnagari: nivedito tārāṃ svabhartre prāyacchad antarvat‍nīmavait patiḥ
translation: The Creator of the Universe (god Brahmā) was then appealed to in this matter by Aṅgiras (Bṛhaspati’s father). Brahmā threatened Soma and made him restore Tārā to her husband who found her to be pregnant.

devanagari: त्यज त्यजाशु दुष्प्रज्ञे मत्क्षेत्रात् आहितं परैः । नाहं त्वां भस्मसात्कुर्यां स्त्रियं सान्तानिकः सति ॥ ९ ॥
english_devnagari: tyaja tyajāśu duṣprajñe matkṣetrāt āhitaṃ paraiḥ nāhaṃ tvāṃ bhasmasātkuryāṃ striyaṃ sāntānikaḥ sati
translation:
(Bṛhaspati ordered Tārā): “Cast off, uproot from my field (i.e. get aborted) what has been sown there by others (or enemies), oh wicked-minded woman. I shall not reduce you to ashes as you are a woman, and I am desirous of having an issue (through you)[4], oh good lady!”
9(A). To her was born a son of golden complexion, the destroyer of Dasyus(or evil beings).
purport:
Footnote [4]:
And as there is no son to ensure the continuity of my family—Bhāgavata Candrikā

devanagari: तत्याज व्रीडिता तारा कुमारं कनकप्रभम् । स्पृहामाङ्गिरसश्चक्रे कुमारे सोम एव च ॥ १० ॥
english_devnagari: tatyāja vrīḍitā tārā kumāraṃ kanakaprabham spṛhāmāṅgirasaścakre kumāre soma eva ca

translation: Overwhelmed with shame, Tārā delivered a son who was lustrous like gold in complexion.[5] And both Bṛhaspati, the son of Aṅgiras, and Soma desired to have the son as his own (as a kṣetraja son—born of his field, viz. wife in the case of the former, and as a vīryaja son—born of his own semen—in the case of the latter)[6]
purport:
Footnote [5]:
v.l. janaka-prabha [prabham]—was like his father in complexion and beauty.

Footnote [6]:
With due respect to these annotators, I fail to understand why god Brahmā took Tārā into confidence to ascertain who was the real father of the child.—The Translator.

devanagari: ममायं न तवेत्युच्चैः तस्मिन् विवदमानयोः । पप्रच्छुः ऋषयो देवा नैवोचे व्रीडिता तु सा ॥ ११ ॥
english_devnagari: mamāyaṃ na tavetyuccaiḥ tasmin vivadamānayoḥ papracchuḥ ṛṣayo devā naivoce vrīḍitā tu sā

[nominative single from √ vrīḍ class 1 verb], [nominative single from √ vrīḍ class 4 verb], [nominative single from √ vrīḍ class 10 verb]

translation: While they (Bṛhaspati and Soma) were wrangling vociferously, “This (son; is mine and not yours”, sages and gods enquired (of Tārā), but through sense of shame, she kept mum.

devanagari: कुमारो मातरं प्राह कुपितोऽलीकलज्जया । किं न वोचस्यसद्वृत्ते आत्मावद्यं वदाशु मे ॥ १२ ॥
english_devnagari: kumāro mātaraṃ prāha kupito kiṃ na vocasyasadvṛtte ātmāvadyaṃ vadāśu me

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [nominative single from √ kup class 4 verb], [nominative single from √ kup class 10 verb]

translation:
Enraged at the sham modesty of his mother (Tārā), the son exploded, “Why don’t you speak, you woman of immoral behaviour? Confess to me immediately your sin”.
12(A).[7] (Although you deserve that punishment) I shall not burn you to ashes (with a curse) for concealing your sinful act (as after all you are my mother). But god Brahmā censured the audacious reviler in her presence.
purport:
Footnote [7]:
This verse is noted by Bhāgavata Candrikā also.

devanagari: ब्रह्मा तां रह आहूय समप्राक्षीच्च सान्त्वयन् । सोमस्येत्याह शनकैः सोमस्तं तावदग्रहीत् ॥ १३ ॥
english_devnagari: brahmā tāṃ raha āhūya samaprākṣīcca sāntvayan somasyetyāha śanakaiḥ somastaṃ tāvadagrahīt

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [nominative single from √ sāntv class 10 verb], [vocative single from √ sāntv class 10 verb]

translation: God Brahmā then invited her aside, and consoling her in camera, he (sympathetically) enquired. With great hesitation and slowly, she confessed in a low tone, “Soma’s.” And immediately Soma took possession of the child.

devanagari: इति श्रीमद्‍भागवते महापुराणे पारमहंस्यां । संहितायां नवमस्कन्धे चतुर्दशोऽध्यायः ॥ १४ ॥
english_devnagari: iti śrīmad‍bhāgavate mahāpurāṇe pāramahaṃsyāṃ saṃhitāyāṃ navamaskandhe caturdaśo
translation: The self-born god (Brahmā) gave the child the name Budha (the intelligent and wise). By the penetrating intelligence and deep wisdom of his son Budha, the moongod, the king of stars, became highly delighted.

devanagari: तस्यात्मयोनिरकृत बुध इत्यभिधां नृप । बुद्ध्या गम्भीरया येन पुत्रेणापोडुराण्मुदम् । ततः पुरूरवा जज्ञे इलायां य उदाहृतः । तस्य रूपगुणौदार...
english_devnagari: tasyātmayonirakṛta budha ityabhidhāṃ nṛpa buddhyā gambhīrayā yena putreṇāpoḍurāṇmudam tataḥ purūravā jajñe ilāyāṃ ya udāhṛtaḥ tasya rūpaguṇaudārya śīladraviṇavikramān

[nominative single from √ śīl class 1 verb], [vocative single from √ śīl class 1 verb], [accusative single from √ śīl class 1 verb]

translation: As already narrated (vide Supra 9.1.35) ‘Purūravas was born of Budha and Ilā. On hearing of the personal charms, excellences, generosity, nobility of character, affluence and deeds of valour as extolled by the celestial sage Nārada in the palace of Indra (the king of gods), the celestial nymph Ūrvaśī was afflicted by shafts of the god of love, and she approached Purūravas.[8]
purport:
Footnote [8]:
This story (of Purūravas and Ūrvaśī) is an elaboration of the dialogue between Purūravas and Ūrvaśī recorded in Ṛg Veda Saṃhitā. 10.95. Many verses from that Sūkta are assimilated verbatim in the Bhāgavata Purāṇa e.g. Ṛg Veda Saṃhitā. 10.95.1, 14, 15. The textual similarity has been noted by all commentators. Padaratnāvalī however is more elaborate in this respect.
Even an ordinary reader will hear the echo of Ṛg Veda Saṃhitā. 10.95.1 (repeated in Śatapatha Brāhmaṇa 11.5.1-6):
haye jāye manasā tiṣṭha ghore /
vacāṃsi miśrā kṛṇavāvahai nu //
In Bh. P. 9.14.34:
aho jāye tiṣṭha tiṣṭha ghore na tyaktum arhasi /
māṃ tvam adyāpyanirvṛtya vacāṃsi kṛṇavāvahai //
If we accept the hypothesis of Rajaram Shastri Bhagawat who, in 1907, showed in his Key to Interpret Veda, that Devas were a race of men and Indra their leader’s designation (and there is no reason to disbelieve it as scholars like R.N. Dandekar, D.D. Kosambi endorsed it independently), and M. Ali’s identification of Kirghizia, Samarkand (Sogdiana of the Greeks) and Turkemenistan with Deva-loka (The Geography of the Purāṇas, pp. 63-83), the contract marriage between a lady from Ūr (Ūr-vaśī) and a king hailing from Ilāvṛta—Aila Purūravas, appears quite a human episode with historical basis. The geographical vicinity of his kingdom to Deva- loka made it possible for him and his descendants to fight as allies of Indra.

devanagari: श्रुत्वोर्वशीन्द्रभवने गीयमानान् सुरर्षिणा । तदन्तिकमुपेयाय देवी स्मरशरार्दिता ॥ १६ ॥
english_devnagari: śrutvorvaśīndrabhavane gīyamānān surarṣiṇā tadantikamupeyāya devī smaraśarārditā

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [accusative plural from √ man class 4 verb], [accusative plural from √ man class 8 verb]

translation: As already narrated (vide Supra 9.1.35) ‘Purūravas was born of Budha and Ilā. On hearing of the personal charms, excellences, generosity, nobility of character, affluence and deeds of valour as extolled by the celestial sage Nārada in the palace of Indra (the king of gods), the celestial nymph Ūrvaśī was afflicted by shafts of the god of love, and she approached Purūravas.[8]
purport:
Footnote [8]:
This story (of Purūravas and Ūrvaśī) is an elaboration of the dialogue between Purūravas and Ūrvaśī recorded in Ṛg Veda Saṃhitā. 10.95. Many verses from that Sūkta are assimilated verbatim in the Bhāgavata Purāṇa e.g. Ṛg Veda Saṃhitā. 10.95.1, 14, 15. The textual similarity has been noted by all commentators. Padaratnāvalī however is more elaborate in this respect.
Even an ordinary reader will hear the echo of Ṛg Veda Saṃhitā. 10.95.1 (repeated in Śatapatha Brāhmaṇa 11.5.1-6):
haye jāye manasā tiṣṭha ghore /
vacāṃsi miśrā kṛṇavāvahai nu //
In Bh. P. 9.14.34:
aho jāye tiṣṭha tiṣṭha ghore na tyaktum arhasi /
māṃ tvam adyāpyanirvṛtya vacāṃsi kṛṇavāvahai //
If we accept the hypothesis of Rajaram Shastri Bhagawat who, in 1907, showed in his Key to Interpret Veda, that Devas were a race of men and Indra their leader’s designation (and there is no reason to disbelieve it as scholars like R.N. Dandekar, D.D. Kosambi endorsed it independently), and M. Ali’s identification of Kirghizia, Samarkand (Sogdiana of the Greeks) and Turkemenistan with Deva-loka (The Geography of the Purāṇas, pp. 63-83), the contract marriage between a lady from Ūr (Ūr-vaśī) and a king hailing from Ilāvṛta—Aila Purūravas, appears quite a human episode with historical basis. The geographical vicinity of his kingdom to Deva- loka made it possible for him and his descendants to fight as allies of Indra.

devanagari: मित्रावरुणयोः शापाद् आपन्ना नरलोकताम् । निशम्य पुरुषश्रेष्ठं कन्दर्पमिव रूपिणम् । धृतिं विष्टभ्य ललना उपतस्थे तदन्तिके ॥ १७ ॥
english_devnagari: mitrāvaruṇayoḥ śāpād āpannā naralokatām niśamya puruṣaśreṣṭhaṃ kandarpamiva rūpiṇam dhṛtiṃ viṣṭabhya lalanā upatasthe tadantike

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [vocative single from √ viṣ class 1 verb], [vocative single from √ viṣ class 3 verb]
[vocative single from √ viṣ class 1 verb], [vocative single from √ viṣ class 3 verb]
[perfect middle first single], [perfect middle third single]

translation: Being reduced to the status of human beings due to the curse of Mitra and Varuṇa, and hearing (the report that) that great personage was beautiful like the god of Love incarnate, she mustered sufficient courage, and presented herself to his presence.

devanagari: स तां विलोक्य नृपतिः हर्षेणोत्फुल्ललोचनः । उवाच श्लक्ष्णया वाचा देवीं हृष्टतनूरुहः ॥ १८ ॥
english_devnagari: sa tāṃ vilokya nṛpatiḥ harṣeṇotphullalocanaḥ uvāca ślakṣṇayā vācā devīṃ hṛṣṭatanūruhaḥ

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [perfect active first single], [perfect active third single]
[perfect active first single], [perfect active third single]

translation: With his eyes fully blooming with great joy at her sight, and with his hair standing on their end (with ecstatic delight), the king spoke to her softly in winsome words.

devanagari: श्रीराजोवाच । स्वागतं ते वरारोहे आस्यतां करवाम किम् । संरमस्व मया साकं रतिर्नौ शाश्वतीः समाः ॥ १९ ॥
english_devnagari: śrīrājovāca svāgataṃ te varārohe āsyatāṃ karavāma kim saṃramasva mayā sākaṃ ratirnau śāśvatīḥ samāḥ

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [perfect active first single], [perfect active third single]
[perfect active first single], [perfect active third single]

translation:
The King said:
Welcome is your arrival, Oh beautiful lady. Be pleased to take a (comfortable) seat. What can we do for you? Enjoy yourself fully with me. Let our mutual enjoyment of love last for innumerable years.

devanagari: उर्वश्युवाच । कस्यास्त्वयि न सज्जेत मनो दृष्टिश्च सुन्दर । यदङ्गान्तरमासाद्य च्यवते ह रिरंसया ॥ २० ॥
english_devnagari: urvaśyuvāca kasyāstvayi na sajjeta mano dṛṣṭiśca sundara yadaṅgāntaramāsādya cyavate ha riraṃsayā

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [perfect active first single], [perfect active third single]
[perfect active first single], [perfect active third single]
[optative active second plural], [optative middle third single]
[nominative single from √ i class 2 verb], [vocative single from √ i class 2 verb], [accusative single from √ i class 2 verb]

translation:
Ūrvaśī replied:
“Whose mind and eye will not be attracted by and fixed on you, oh charming Prince? Having caught a glimpse of your bosom (lit. having approached your bosom) I become overpowered with the desire of enjoying life with you, and lose the control over my mind.

devanagari: एतौ उरणकौ राजन् न्यासौ रक्षस्व मानद । संरंस्ये भवता साकं श्लाघ्यः स्त्रीणां वरः स्मृतः ॥ २१ ॥
english_devnagari: etau uraṇakau rājan nyāsau rakṣasva mānada saṃraṃsye bhavatā sākaṃ ślāghyaḥ strīṇāṃ varaḥ smṛtaḥ

translation: Oh King! Be pleased to protect the two lambs as my pledges. Oh respector of the respectable! I shall enjoy life fully with you. For he who (by his qualities of beauty etc.) appears praise-worthy (to a woman) is remembered (ordained in the Smṛti texts) as acceptable to women.

devanagari: घृतं मे वीर भक्ष्यं स्यात् नेक्षे त्वान्यत्र मैथुनात् । विवाससं तत् तथेति प्रतिपेदे महामनाः ॥ २२ ॥
english_devnagari: ghṛtaṃ me vīra bhakṣyaṃ syāt nekṣe tvānyatra maithunāt vivāsasaṃ tat tatheti pratipede mahāmanāḥ

translation: Oh warrior King! My food will consist of ghee only. And I shall not see you without clothes except on the occasion of sexual intercourse.” The noble-minded king agreed to the conditions.

devanagari: अहो रूपमहो भावो नरलोकविमोहनम् । को न सेवेत मनुजो देवीं त्वां स्वयमागताम् ॥ २३ ॥
english_devnagari: aho rūpamaho bhāvo naralokavimohanam ko na seveta manujo devīṃ tvāṃ svayamāgatām
translation: “Oh what elegant form! How noble sentiment, that enchants the entire humanity! What man will not attend upon a goddess who presents herself of her own accord.”

devanagari: तया स पुरुषश्रेष्ठो रमयन्त्या यथार्हतः । रेमे सुरविहारेषु कामं चैत्ररथादिषु ॥ २४ ॥
english_devnagari: tayā sa puruṣaśreṣṭho ramayantyā yathārhataḥ reme suravihāreṣu kāmaṃ caitrarathādiṣu

translation: In her company she offered him all pleasures worthy of him. The eminent person (king Purūravas) enjoyed with her at will, and to the full, in the pleasure gardens of gods such as Caitraratha (the garden of Kubera).

devanagari: रममाणस्तया देव्या पद्मकिञ्जल्क गन्धया । तन्मुखामोदमुषितो मुमुदेऽहर्गणान् बहून् ॥ २५ ॥
english_devnagari: ramamāṇastayā devyā padmakiñjalka gandhayā tanmukhāmodamuṣito mumude

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [perfect middle first single], [perfect middle third single]

translation: Enjoying amorous pleasures with the goddess (or the queen) whose person was fragrant like lotus-filaments, and being enraptured by the sweet smell of her mouth, he revelled many years delightfully with her.

devanagari: अपश्यन् उर्वशीं इन्द्रो गन्धर्वान् समचोदयत् । उर्वशीरहितं मह्यं आस्थानं नातिशोभते ॥ २६ ॥
english_devnagari: apaśyan urvaśīṃ indro gandharvān samacodayat urvaśīrahitaṃ mahyaṃ āsthānaṃ nātiśobhate

translation: Not seeing Ūrvaśī (in his court) and feeling, “My assembly does not look very charming without Ūrvaśī,” Indra directed Gandharvas (to bring her).

devanagari: ते उपेत्य महारात्रे तमसि प्रत्युपस्थिते । उर्वश्या उरणौ जह्रुः न्यस्तौ राजनि जायया ॥ २७ ॥
english_devnagari: te upetya mahārātre tamasi pratyupasthite urvaśyā uraṇau jahruḥ nyastau rājani jāyayā
translation: Approaching at dead of night when the world was steeped in darkness, and through their Māyā, they stole the two rams which were pledged for safe custody with the king, by his queen Ūrvaśī.

devanagari: निशम्याक्रन्दितं देवी पुत्रयोः नीयमानयोः । हतास्म्यहं कुनाथेन नपुंसा वीरमानिना ॥ २८ ॥
english_devnagari: niśamyākranditaṃ devī putrayoḥ nīyamānayoḥ hatāsmyahaṃ kunāthena napuṃsā vīramāninā

translation: Hearing the bleating of her son-like rams, as they were being carried away, queen Ūrvaśī lamented loudly, “I am undone by this sheepish husband, lacking in manliness but posing vaingloriously as a hero.

devanagari: यद् विश्रम्भादहं नष्टा हृतापत्या च दस्युभिः । यः शेते निशि संत्रस्तो यथा नारी दिवा पुमान् ॥ २९ ॥
english_devnagari: yad viśrambhādahaṃ naṣṭā hṛtāpatyā ca dasyubhiḥ yaḥ śete niśi saṃtrasto yathā nārī divā pumān

translation:
Trusting in him, I am ruined and despoiled of my children (—like lambs) by robbers. Like a woman who is full of fear at night, he sleeps terror-stricken at night, and poses himself as a man when it is (broad) day (—light).
29(A). Let me be free! Leave me immediately! Take away your arm that clasps around me. He is too timid to leave me, and cunning, and addicted to licentious sexual pleasure only.”

devanagari: इति वाक् सायकैर्बिद्धः प्रतोत्त्रैरिव कुञ्जरः । निशि निस्त्रिंशमादाय विवस्त्रोऽभ्यद्रवद् रुषा ॥ ३० ॥
english_devnagari: iti vāk sāyakairbiddhaḥ pratottrairiva kuñjaraḥ niśi nistriṃśamādāya vivastro
translation: Being pierced with these verbal shafts, and like an elephant pricked with goads, he caught hold of his sword and sallied forth in rage, in the dead of night, even though he was naked.

devanagari: ते विसृज्योरणौ तत्र व्यद्योतन्त स्म विद्युतः । आदाय मेषावायान्तं नग्नमैक्षत सा पतिम् ॥ ३१ ॥
english_devnagari: te visṛjyoraṇau tatra vyadyotanta sma vidyutaḥ ādāya meṣāvāyāntaṃ nagnamaikṣata sā patim

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [imperfect middle third single], [aorist middle third plural]

translation: The Gandharvas let go the two rams, but flashed out light like lightning, when she caught a glimpse of her husband in a nude state, coming with the two rams (and thus left him).

devanagari: ऐलोऽपि शयने जायां अपश्यन् विमना इव । तच्चित्तो विह्वलः शोचन् बभ्रामोन्मत्तवन् महीम् ॥ ३२ ॥
english_devnagari: ailo taccitto vihvalaḥ śocan babhrāmonmattavan mahīm

translation: Not finding his wife in the bed, Purūravas, the son of Ilā, became despondent. His heart being fixed on her, he was beside himself (with affliction). He ranged over the world (in search of her), like a mad man.

devanagari: स तां वीक्ष्य कुरुक्षेत्रे सरस्वत्यां च तत्सखीः । पञ्च प्रहृष्टवदनाः प्राह सूक्तं पुरूरवाः ॥ ३३ ॥
english_devnagari: sa tāṃ vīkṣya kurukṣetre sarasvatyāṃ ca tatsakhīḥ pañca prahṛṣṭavadanāḥ prāha sūktaṃ purūravāḥ
translation: (In the course of his wanderings, one day) he happened to see her along with her five female companions with their countenances blooming with delight, on the bank of the Sarasvatī, in Kurukṣetra. Purūravas addressed her the following (persuasive) charming words (recorded in the Sūkta, Ṛg Veda Saṃhitā 10.95 which is adapted here as follows):

devanagari: १ अहो जाये तिष्ठ तिष्ठ घोरे न त्यक्तुमर्हसि । मां त्वमद्याप्यनिर्वृत्य वचांसि कृणवावहै ॥ ३४ ॥
english_devnagari: 1 aho jāye tiṣṭha tiṣṭha ghore na tyaktumarhasi māṃ tvamadyāpyanirvṛtya vacāṃsi kṛṇavāvahai
translation: “Oh my beloved wife! Do stop awhile, wait. Oh cruel-hearted one! It does not behove you to abandon me without giving me pleasure of your company to my satisfaction (or unsated as I am of the pleasures of your company, you ought not to relegate me to terrible misery). Let us (at least) have some (heart to heart) talk.[9]

devanagari: सुदेहोऽयं पतत्यत्र देवि दूरं हृतस्त्वया । खादन्त्येनं वृका गृध्राः त्वत्प्रसादस्य नास्पदम् ॥ ३५ ॥
english_devnagari: sudeho khādantyenaṃ vṛkā gṛdhrāḥ tvatprasādasya nāspadam

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [nominative dual from √ pat class 1 verb], [vocative dual from √ pat class 1 verb], [accusative dual from √ pat class 1 verb], [locative single from √ pat class 1 verb]

translation: If this beautiful body of mine[10] which has been drawn away to such a far off distance by you, be not the recipient of your favours, it will fall down dead here only, and wolves and vultures will prey upon it, Oh my queen (Ob goddess).
purport:
Footnote [10]:
Cf. Ṛg Veda Saṃhitā. 10.95.14. Padaratnāvalī reads ‘sudevo'yam’ for sudeho'yam, which opens Ṛg Veda Saṃhitā 10.95.14 (sudevo atra prapatet). Padaratnāvalī explains the Ṛk rather than this verse Sudeva—This human body which is the tool or means of getting knowledge (gati-lakṣaṇa-jñāna-sādhanam).
Bhāgavata Candrikā explains the v.l. sudevo’yam: Here Purūravas addresses his own soul—the Inner Controller. He construes gṛdhra (greedy, voracious) as the adj. qualifying Vṛkas—wolves in the form of sense-organs which afflict the individual soul.

devanagari: उर्वश्युवाच । मा मृथाः पुरुषोऽसि त्वं मा स्म त्वाद्युर्वृका इमे । क्वापि सख्यं न वै स्त्रीणां वृकाणां हृदयं यथा ॥ ३६ ॥
english_devnagari: urvaśyuvāca mā mṛthāḥ puruṣo kvāpi sakhyaṃ na vai strīṇāṃ vṛkāṇāṃ hṛdayaṃ yathā

translation:
Ūrvaśī replied:
Do not die. You are a man (as such you must be courageous). Let not these wolves (in the form of passionately craving senses) devour you today (Control your senses). The heart of women like the heart of wolves is friendly to none.[11]
purport:
Footnote [11]:
The veres echoes the text of Ṛg Veda Saṃhitā. 10.95.15 while VV. 36-39 explain it. Cf.
purū-ravo mā mṛthā, mā pra papto
mā tvā vṛkāso aśivāsa u kṣan /
na vai straiṇāni sakhyāni santi
sālāvṛkāṇām hṛdayāneytā //—Ṛg Veda Saṃhitā. 10.95.15

devanagari: स्त्रियो ह्यकरुणाः क्रूरा दुर्मर्षाः प्रियसाहसाः । घ्नन्त्यल्पार्थेऽपि विश्रब्धं पतिं भ्रातरमप्युत ॥ ३७ ॥
english_devnagari: striyo hyakaruṇāḥ krūrā durmarṣāḥ priyasāhasāḥ ghnantyalpārthe

translation: Women are merciless, crud, jealous and mind no risk for the object of their love. Even for a trifling matter, they will kill their trustful husband or unsuspecting brother.

devanagari: विधायालीकविश्रम्भं अज्ञेषु त्यक्तसौहृदाः । नवं नवमभीप्सन्त्यः पुंश्चल्यः स्वैरवृत्तयः ॥ ३८ ॥

translation: After inspiring confidence in gullible persons (through deceitful behaviour), they snap asunder all friendly relations. The unchaste women, being wantonly wayward in behaviour, hunt (long) after a new lover (every now and then).

devanagari: संवत्सरान्ते हि भवान् एकरात्रं मयेश्वरः । वस्यत्यपत्यानि च ते भविष्यन्त्यपराणि भोः ॥ ३९ ॥
english_devnagari: saṃvatsarānte hi bhavān ekarātraṃ mayeśvaraḥ vasyatyapatyāni ca te bhaviṣyantyaparāṇi bhoḥ

translation: My lord, at the end of the year, you will stay with me for one night. You will have other children also, (in addition to the one I carry).

devanagari: अन्तर्वत्‍नीमुपालक्ष्य देवीं स प्रययौ पुरीम् । पुनस्तत्र गतोऽब्दान्ते उर्वशीं वीरमातरम् ॥ ४० ॥
english_devnagari: antarvat‍nīmupālakṣya devīṃ sa prayayau purīm punastatra gato
translation: Noticing that the queen was pregnant, he returned to his capital. At the end of the year, he came back again to find Ūrvaśī, as the mother of a hero.

devanagari: उपलभ्य मुदा युक्तः समुवास तया निशाम् । अथैनमुर्वशी प्राह कृपणं विरहातुरम् ॥ ४१ ॥

translation: Full of delight to have her again, he spent that night with her. Then finding him disheartened at the prospect of impending separation, Ūrvaśī advised him:

devanagari: गन्धर्वान् उपधावेमान् तुभ्यं दास्यन्ति मामिति । तस्य संस्तुवतस्तुष्टा अग्निस्थालीं ददुर्नृप । उर्वशीं मन्यमानस्तां सोऽबुध्यत चरन् वने ॥...

translation:
“You propitiate these Gandharvas (by praise), and they will be pleased to give me unto you.”
Being pleased with his laudatory prayer, the Gandharvas donated him a fire-vase (for fire-worship which will lead him to the region of Ūrvaśī) Oh King. (Being blinded with passion) he believed that fire-vessel to be (the means of securing) Ūrvaśī, and roved in the forest with it, only to discover it to be a vase for carrying fire, and not Ūrvaśī.

devanagari: स्थालीं न्यस्य वने गत्वा गृहानाध्यायतो निशि । त्रेतायां सम्प्रवृत्तायां मनसि त्रय्यवर्तत ॥ ४३ ॥
english_devnagari: sthālīṃ nyasya vane gatvā gṛhānādhyāyato niśi tretāyāṃ sampravṛttāyāṃ manasi trayyavartata

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [imperfect active second plural], [imperfect middle third single]

translation: Depositing the vase in the forest, he returned to his palace, and contemplated throughout the night on Ūrvaśī. In the meanwhile (the Kṛta age ended and) the Tretā Age set in, and the knowledge of trinity of Vedas which contained prescriptions, for the ritualistic performance of sacrifices, revealed itself in his mind.

devanagari: स्थालीस्थानं गतोऽश्वत्थं शमीगर्भं विलक्ष्य सः । तेन द्वे अरणी कृत्वा उर्वशीलोककाम्यया ॥ ४४ ॥
english_devnagari: sthālīsthānaṃ gato tena dve araṇī kṛtvā urvaśīlokakāmyayā
translation: Returning to the spot (in the forest) where the firevase was kept (by him), he noticed an aśvattha (Peepal) tree grown out from the ‘womb’ of the Śamī tree. With a desire to attain to the region of Ūrvaśī, he made two araṇis (churning sticks) out of them (with a view to enkindle fire by friction).

devanagari: उर्वशीं मन्त्रतो ध्यायन् अधरारणिमुत्तराम् । आत्मानं उभयोर्मध्ये यत्तत् प्रजननं प्रभुः ॥ ४५ ॥
english_devnagari: urvaśīṃ mantrato dhyāyan adharāraṇimuttarām ātmānaṃ ubhayormadhye yattat prajananaṃ prabhuḥ

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [nominative single from √ i class 2 verb], [vocative single from √ i class 2 verb], [accusative single from √ i class 2 verb]

translation: Reciting the mantra ‘ūrvaśyām urasi purūravāḥ’ (Purūravas is on Ūrvaśī), the powerful king contemplated the lower piece of araṇi (churning stick) as Ūrvaśī and the upper araṇi to be himself, and the intermediate piece between them as their issue (son), (the king churned out the fire while saying the mantra prescribed for such frictional fire).

devanagari: तस्य निर्मन्थनात् जातो जातवेदा विभावसुः । त्रय्या स विद्यया राज्ञा पुत्रत्वे कल्पितस्त्रिवृत् ॥ ४६ ॥
english_devnagari: tasya nirmanthanāt jāto jātavedā vibhāvasuḥ trayyā sa vidyayā rājñā putratve kalpitastrivṛt

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [nominative single from √ jan class 1 verb], [nominative single from √ jan class 2 verb], [nominative single from √ jan class 3 verb], [nominative single from √ jan class 4 verb]

translation: By that friction of churning was enkindled a fire (flame) which is called Jātaveda (that from which wealth and every enjoyable object is produced). It was consecrated by the method prescribed in the three Vedas. It assumed three forms[12]—āhavanīya, gārhapatya and dakṣiṇāgni. As it, with its three forms, leads to the celestial regions, it was adopted as his son by the king Purūravas.
purport:
Footnote [12]:
These are the three forms of householder’s perpetual sacrificial fires: The āhavanīya is the eastern. The Gārhapatya is another perpetual fire transmitted from one generation to another and from this, fires for sacrificial purposes are lighted; the dakṣiṇāgni is the sacred fire placed to the south.

devanagari: तेनायजत यज्ञेशं भगवन्तं अधोक्षजम् । उर्वशीलोकमन्विच्छन् सर्वदेवमयं हरिम् ॥ ४७ ॥
english_devnagari: tenāyajata yajñeśaṃ bhagavantaṃ adhokṣajam urvaśīlokamanvicchan sarvadevamayaṃ harim

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [nominative single from √ iṣ class 6 verb], [vocative single from √ iṣ class 6 verb]

translation: Wishing to attain to the region of Ūrvaśī he, (Purūravas), worshipped with that sacrificial fire (produced by attrition from araṇis) the glorious Lord Hari, who is the presiding Deity of sacrifices and is Supra-sensuous and who represents all gods in His Person.

devanagari: एक एव पुरा वेदः प्रणवः सर्ववाङ्मयः । देवो नारायणो नान्य एकोऽग्निर्वर्ण एव च ॥ ४८ ॥
english_devnagari: eka eva purā vedaḥ praṇavaḥ sarvavāṅmayaḥ devo nārāyaṇo nānya eko
translation: Formerly (in the Kṛta Age), there was only one Veda viz. the sacred syllable OM which formed the basis of all speech; there was but one God Nārāyaṇa and no other; there was only one Fire (and not three) and only one caste viz. Haṃsa.

devanagari: पुरूरवस एवासीत् त्वयी त्रेतामुखे नृप । अग्निना प्रजया राजा लोकं गान्धर्वमेयिवान् ॥ ४९ ॥
english_devnagari: purūravasa evāsīt tvayī tretāmukhe nṛpa agninā prajayā rājā lokaṃ gāndharvameyivān

analyses.morphological_and_syntactical_analysis: [aorist active third single], [injunctive active third single]

translation:
(In the Kṛta Age), all people had the predominance of Sattva and hence were devoted to spiritual meditation. In the Tretā Age, rajas being the dominant quality, the path of ritualistic acts based on the three Vedas became evolved. Since the time of Purūravas, when the Tretā Age dawned, there came into existence the trinity of the Vedas (and the path of ritualistic action based on them), Oh King. As the King accepted the Fire-god as his son (i.e. maintained the sacrificial fire like unto his son), the king attained to the realm of Gandharvas.
